original_hypothesis.title: Reentrancy in Core minting via ERC721Receiver callback may allow unexpected nested mints/airdrops and break supply/allowance assumptions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: NextGenCore._mintProcessing uses _safeMint, which triggers onERC721Received on contract recipients. During this callback, the recipient can call back into other public Core functions (e.g., burn) or external Minter functions that might be callable, potentially creating nested state transitions while the mint flow is in-progress. Combined with the already-fragile circulationSupply accounting and external randomizer calls, this could lead to inconsistent mint limits or supply corruption.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete reentrancy surface because _safeMint is an external callback into an untrusted contract mid-mint. While mint() itself is only callable by minterContract, the callback can still interact with other public entry points (burn, metadata views) and may interact with the Minter contract (mint/burnToMint) if any path permits.
deployment_assumptions: MinterContract.mint allows _mintTo to be an arbitrary contract that implements onERC721Received
There exists at least one callable path during callback that can cause additional mints/airdrops/burn-to-mints in the same transaction (directly or indirectly)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No production wiring evidence beyond test fixtures; mint() allows arbitrary _mintTo; Core uses _safeMint callbacks by design.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _mintProcessing calls _safeMint (external callback) after writing tokenData and tokenIdsToCollectionIds and after calling external randomizer.calculateTokenHash

preconditions: Recipient _mintTo is a contract implementing onERC721Received
The callback reenters into some state-changing function(s) on Core/Minter/Randomizer that are reachable with current msg.sender
Any reentered function sequence creates an observable accounting or state violation (supply, mint limits, token data)

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: NextGenCore.sol _mintProcessing uses _safeMint with no nonReentrant guard; external call to randomizer happens before _safeMint
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external callback during sensitive state transition
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Nested calls could exploit missing atomic assumptions (e.g., supply counters, per-address limits tracked in Core)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No reentrancy guard; no explicit minting lock; no checks-effects-interactions discipline around callback

analysis_questions: During onERC721Received, can the recipient call MinterContract.mint/burnToMint/burnOrSwapExternalToMint in a way that passes checks (msg.sender changes)?
Can the recipient call Core.burn on the just-minted token within the callback and create inconsistent burnAmount/totalSupplyOfCollection behavior?
Do any functions rely on intermediate state that could be observed/manipulated mid-mint?
validation_requirements: Build ERC721Receiver that reenters into Core.burn and any reachable Minter function; fuzz sequences
Assert invariants: circulationSupply vs minted count, burnAmount correctness, per-address mint counts
Check if any privileged checks mistakenly allow callback contract to escalate actions

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker mints to a malicious ERC721Receiver contract. In onERC721Received, it immediately calls Core.burn on the received token and/or triggers further mint flows (if reachable), attempting to desynchronize accounting and create phantom supply/burn states that block others or skew pricing.
prerequisites: Ability to mint at least one token to a contract address

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High: could brick collection minting, bypass intended per-address limits, or corrupt supply/burn accounting
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All minters in affected collection; marketplace users relying on scarcity/supply reporting

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Maybe (reentrant depth and gas stipend during callback)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Track nested call traces and state deltas within one transaction

validation_result.mechanism: Reentrancy via _safeMint callback bypassing mint limits
validation_result.impact_summary: Attackers can bypass per-address mint limits (maxAllowance) and time-gated minting rules (salesOption 3) to mint unlimited tokens in a single transaction.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The vulnerability stems from a violation of the Checks-Effects-Interactions (CEI) pattern combined with the use of `_safeMint`. In `NextGenCore.mint`, the state variables tracking how many tokens an address has minted (`tokensMintedPerAddress` and `tokensMintedAllowlistAddress`) are incremented *after* the call to `_mintProcessing`. `_mintProcessing` invokes `_safeMint`, which triggers the `onERC721Received` callback on the recipient address. Because the `_mintTo` address is user-controlled in `MinterContract.mint`, an attacker can supply a malicious smart contract. During the `onERC721Received` callback, the attacker's contract can reenter `MinterContract.mint`. Since the mint tracking variables in `NextGenCore` have not yet been updated, the reentrant call will pass the `maxAllowance` checks. This allows the attacker to recursively mint tokens until the collection's total supply is exhausted. Similarly, in `MinterContract.mint`, the `lastMintDate` for `salesOption == 3` (which enforces 1 mint per time period) is updated *after* the minting loop, allowing the time-gate to be bypassed via the same reentrancy vector.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows state updates occurring after external calls. `NextGenCore.mint` updates `tokensMintedPerAddress` after `_mintProcessing`. `MinterContract.mint` updates `lastMintDate` after calling `gencore.mint`. Neither contract employs reentrancy guards on these critical functions.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The state variables tracking the number of tokens minted by an address are updated after `_mintProcessing`, which triggers an external call via `_safeMint`. This allows a reentrant call to bypass allowance checks.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
require(_numberOfTokens <= gencore.viewMaxAllowance(col), "Change no of tokens");
            require(gencore.retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress(col, msg.sender) + _numberOfTokens <= gencore.viewMaxAllowance(col), "Max");
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This check relies on `retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress`, which returns stale data during a reentrant call because the core contract hasn't updated the counter yet.
For salesOption 3, `lastMintDate` is updated at the very end of the function. A reentrant call during the minting process will see the old `lastMintDate` and bypass the 1 mint/period restriction.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: An attacker calls `MinterContract.mint` with a malicious contract as `_mintTo`. The allowance check passes, and `NextGenCore.mint` is called. `NextGenCore.mint` calls `_mintProcessing`, which calls `_safeMint`. This triggers `onERC721Received` on the attacker's contract. The attacker's contract reenters `MinterContract.mint`. Because `tokensMintedPerAddress` in `NextGenCore` has not yet been incremented (it happens after `_mintProcessing` returns), the allowance check passes again. The attacker can repeat this to mint beyond the allowed limit.

deployment_assumptions_checked: MinterContract.mint allows _mintTo to be an arbitrary contract that implements onERC721Received
There exists at least one callable path during callback that can cause additional mints/airdrops/burn-to-mints in the same transaction (directly or indirectly)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The `mint` function in `MinterContract.sol` explicitly takes `address _mintTo` as a parameter and passes it to `gencore.mint`, which uses it in `_safeMint`. There are no reentrancy guards preventing `MinterContract.mint` from being called again.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The preliminary attack scenario is highly realistic and directly exploitable. An attacker deploys a smart contract that implements `onERC721Received`. In the callback, the contract calls `MinterContract.mint` again. By doing so, the attacker can mint the entire collection supply in a single transaction, completely bypassing `maxAllowance` limits and time-based minting restrictions, provided they have enough ETH to cover the minting costs.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High. This vulnerability completely undermines the fairness of the minting process. Attackers can hoard rare NFTs, bypass allowlist limits, and defeat time-gated distribution mechanisms. This can lead to severe reputational damage and financial loss for legitimate users who are front-run or blocked from minting.
recommended_mitigations: Implement the Checks-Effects-Interactions (CEI) pattern in `NextGenCore.mint` and `NextGenCore.airDropTokens` by updating `tokensMintedPerAddress` and `tokensMintedAllowlistAddress` BEFORE calling `_mintProcessing`.

Add a `nonReentrant` modifier (e.g., from OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard) to all state-changing external functions in `MinterContract`, especially `mint`, `burnToMint`, and `burnOrSwapExternalToMint`.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy NextGenCore and MinterContract. 2. Configure a collection with a maxAllowance of 1. 3. Deploy an AttackerContract that implements `onERC721Received`. 4. In `onERC721Received`, check if a target mint count is reached; if not, call `MinterContract.mint` again. 5. Call `MinterContract.mint` via the AttackerContract. 6. Assert that the AttackerContract successfully minted multiple tokens, exceeding the maxAllowance of 1.
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test the reentrancy depth to ensure block gas limits are the only bounding factor.
Verify if similar reentrancy vectors exist in `burnOrSwapExternalToMint` via the external ERC721 `safeTransferFrom` callback.
